$comment: Puzzle answers (quotations to be enciphered). SPEC §4. Each puzzle carries a category and a hint (TODO-004); the attribution is enciphered alongside the quote and revealed on solve.

puzzles.text: Duchovny does not deserve treats nor a spot on the couch.
puzzles.hint: A stern verdict on a rival cat.

puzzles.text: The unexamined life is not worth living.
puzzles.hint: A classical Greek philosopher on the value of self-reflection.

puzzles.text: I think, therefore I am.
puzzles.hint: An early modern rationalist's proof of his own existence.

puzzles.text: It does not matter how slowly you go as long as you do not stop.
puzzles.hint: An ancient Chinese sage on perseverance.

puzzles.text: That which does not kill us makes us stronger.
puzzles.hint: A 19th-century German philosopher on surviving adversity.

puzzles.text: Knowing yourself is the beginning of all wisdom.
puzzles.hint: A classical Greek philosopher on self-knowledge.

puzzles.text: The measure of a man is what he does with power.
puzzles.hint: An ancient Greek thinker on how authority reveals character.

puzzles.text: Patience is bitter, but its fruit is sweet.
puzzles.hint: A classical Greek philosopher on enduring for a later reward.

puzzles.text: Real knowledge is to know the extent of one's ignorance.
puzzles.hint: An ancient Chinese sage on recognizing the boundary of what we grasp.

puzzles.text: He who has a why to live can bear almost any how.
puzzles.hint: A 19th-century German philosopher on purpose making hardship endurable.

puzzles.text: There is only one good, knowledge, and one evil, ignorance.
puzzles.hint: A classical Greek philosopher equating a single virtue with a single flaw.

puzzles.text: It is not enough to have a good mind; the main thing is to use it well.
puzzles.hint: An early modern rationalist on the importance of applying one's reason.

puzzles.text: We are not rich by what we possess but by what we can do without.
puzzles.hint: An Enlightenment thinker on freedom from material craving.

puzzles.text: It is not what happens to you, but how you react to it that matters.
puzzles.hint: A Stoic teacher on choosing our attitude toward circumstance.

puzzles.text: Luck is what happens when preparation meets opportunity.
puzzles.hint: A Roman Stoic on why fortune favors the ready.

puzzles.text: The journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step.
puzzles.hint: An ancient Chinese sage on how vast undertakings get underway.

puzzles.text: To play a wrong note is insignificant; to play without passion is inexcusable.
puzzles.hint: A towering deaf German composer on valuing feeling over mere accuracy.

puzzles.text: If you have to ask what jazz is, you'll never know.
puzzles.hint: A gravelly-voiced trumpet great on how improvised swing defies explanation.

puzzles.text: The aim and final end of all music should be none other than the glory of God.
puzzles.hint: A Baroque master of counterpoint on the sacred purpose of composition.

puzzles.text: The music is not in the notes, but in the silence between.
puzzles.hint: A child-prodigy Classical composer on what lies in the pauses.

puzzles.text: A problem is a chance for you to do your best.
puzzles.hint: A jazz bandleader and pianist reframing difficulty as opportunity.

puzzles.text: Inspiration is a guest that does not willingly visit the lazy.
puzzles.hint: A Romantic Russian composer on why effort precedes the muse.

puzzles.text: Do not fear mistakes. There are none.
puzzles.hint: A pioneering jazz trumpeter urging boldness in improvisation.

puzzles.text: Simplicity is the final achievement.
puzzles.hint: A Romantic pianist-composer prizing the hard-won plainness of art.

puzzles.text: The more constraints one imposes, the more one frees oneself.
puzzles.hint: A revolutionary modernist composer on the paradox of creative limits.

puzzles.text: Music is nothing separate from me. It is me.
puzzles.hint: A soul and R&B pioneer describing his art as identity itself.

puzzles.text: Swing low, sweet chariot, coming for to carry me home.
puzzles.hint: A spiritual longing for deliverance and rest beyond this life.

puzzles.text: Amazing grace, how sweet the sound, that saved a wretch like me.
puzzles.hint: A beloved hymn of redemption and undeserved mercy.

puzzles.text: The summer's gone, and all the roses falling.
puzzles.hint: A wistful Irish ballad of farewell and waiting.

puzzles.text: Home, home on the range, where the deer and the antelope play.
puzzles.hint: An American West anthem of open prairie contentment.

puzzles.text: Are you going to Scarborough Fair? Parsley, sage, rosemary and thyme.
puzzles.hint: A traditional English ballad listing herbs as a refrain.

puzzles.text: The water is wide, I cannot cross over.
puzzles.hint: A folk lament on love separated by distance.

puzzles.text: Oh Shenandoah, I long to hear you, away you rolling river.
puzzles.hint: An American sea shanty pining for a far western valley.

puzzles.text: Should auld acquaintance be forgot, and never brought to mind.
puzzles.hint: A Scottish New Year toast to old friendships.

puzzles.text: Oh, when the saints go marching in, I want to be in that number.
puzzles.attribution: When the Saints Go Marching In
puzzles.hint: A New Orleans gospel standard about joining the blessed procession.

puzzles.text: Alas, my love, you do me wrong to cast me off discourteously.
puzzles.hint: A Renaissance English tune mourning a cruel rejection.

puzzles.text: For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son.
puzzles.hint: The gospel's summary of divine sacrifice for humanity.

puzzles.text: Trust in the Lord with all your heart and lean not on your own understanding.
puzzles.hint: A call to rely on God rather than personal insight.

puzzles.text: The Lord is my shepherd, I lack nothing.
puzzles.hint: A comforting image of divine provision and care.

puzzles.text: In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth.
puzzles.hint: The opening declaration of creation itself.

puzzles.text: Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called children of God.
puzzles.hint: A beatitude honoring those who reconcile others.

puzzles.text: But those who hope in the Lord will renew their strength.
puzzles.hint: A promise of restored vigor for the faithful.

puzzles.text: I can do all this through him who gives me strength.
puzzles.hint: An affirmation of empowerment from a higher source.

puzzles.text: Do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good.
puzzles.hint: A charge to conquer wrongdoing through virtue.

puzzles.text: There is a time for everything, and a season for every activity under the heavens.
puzzles.hint: A meditation on the proper moment for all things.

puzzles.text: Love is patient, love is kind. It does not envy, it does not boast.
puzzles.hint: A famous description of selfless devotion.

puzzles.text: Be the change you wish to see in the world.
puzzles.hint: An Indian independence leader urging us to embody our ideals.

puzzles.text: The future belongs to those who believe in the beauty of their dreams.
puzzles.hint: A pioneering American first lady on hope and aspiration.

puzzles.text: The secret of getting ahead is getting started.
puzzles.hint: An American humorist and novelist on the value of beginning.

puzzles.text: Be yourself; everyone else is already taken.
puzzles.hint: An Irish wit and playwright championing authenticity.

puzzles.text: People will never forget how you made them feel.
puzzles.hint: An American poet and memoirist on emotional impact.

puzzles.text: Success is not final, failure is not fatal: it is the courage to continue that counts.
puzzles.hint: A wartime British prime minister on perseverance through setbacks.

puzzles.text: Your time is limited, so do not waste it living someone else's life.
puzzles.hint: A visionary technology founder on not squandering your days.

puzzles.text: It always seems impossible until it is done.
puzzles.hint: An anti-apartheid icon and South African president on persistence.

puzzles.text: Imagination is more important than knowledge.
puzzles.hint: A theoretical physicist prizing creativity over mere facts.

puzzles.text: Believe you can and you are halfway there.
puzzles.hint: An energetic American president on the power of self-confidence.

puzzles.text: Chess is war over the board. The object is to crush the opponent's mind.
puzzles.hint: An American world champion on the mental battle across the checkered squares.

puzzles.text: Chess is mental torture.
puzzles.hint: A dominant Soviet-born grandmaster on the game's psychological strain.

puzzles.text: When you see a good move, look for a better one.
puzzles.hint: A long-reigning German champion urging patience before committing.

puzzles.text: The blunders are all there on the board, waiting to be made.
puzzles.hint: A witty grandmaster on mistakes lurking in every position.

puzzles.text: A win by an unsound combination, however showy, fills me with artistic horror.
puzzles.hint: The first official world champion prizing soundness over flashy play.

puzzles.text: The threat is stronger than the execution.
puzzles.hint: A hypermodern theorist on the power of looming menace.

puzzles.text: Chess, like love, like music, has the power to make people happy.
puzzles.hint: A classical German master comparing the game to joyful arts.

puzzles.text: You may learn much more from a game you lose than from a game you win.
puzzles.hint: A Cuban prodigy on the instructive value of defeat.

puzzles.text: You must take your opponent into a deep dark forest where two plus two equals five.
puzzles.hint: An attacking Latvian genius famed for bewildering complications.

puzzles.text: During a chess competition a chessmaster should be a combination of a beast of prey and a monk.
puzzles.hint: A fierce world champion blending predatory focus with ascetic discipline.

puzzles.text: When the power of love overcomes the love of power, the world will know peace.
puzzles.hint: A revolutionary electric virtuoso on harmony triumphing over dominance.

puzzles.text: The beautiful thing about learning is that no one can take it away from you.
puzzles.hint: A blues legend on knowledge as a lasting personal treasure.

puzzles.text: I've never been able to explain what happens when I play. It just happens.
puzzles.hint: A celebrated bluesman describing performance as instinctive mystery.

puzzles.text: The guitar is a small orchestra. It is polyphonic. Every string is a different color.
puzzles.hint: A classical maestro who elevated the six-stringed instrument to concert respect.

puzzles.text: I never play the same thing twice. I never play the same solo twice.
puzzles.hint: A gypsy jazz pioneer prizing spontaneous, ever-changing improvisation.

puzzles.text: The guitar is the easiest instrument to play badly and the hardest to play well.
puzzles.hint: An inventor of the solid-body electric on deceptive simplicity.

puzzles.text: Don't let the same dog bite you twice.
puzzles.hint: A rock and roll founding father warning against repeated folly.

puzzles.text: The most valuable possession you can own is an open heart.
puzzles.hint: A Latin-rock guitarist on compassion as true wealth.

puzzles.text: Take the time to make your soul happy.
puzzles.hint: A Texas blues phenomenon on nurturing inner contentment.

puzzles.text: Music is a language that doesn't speak in particular words. It speaks in emotions.
puzzles.hint: A Rolling Stones riff-master on melody's wordless power.
